sections.title: Microsoft 365 Alerts
items: Multiple successful logins from different countries within 15 minutes

sections.title: Additional Findings
items: OAuth token remains active after password reset

questions.question: What MOST likely occurred?
options: Patch failure on the mail server
questions.explanation: Multiple impossible-travel logins, OAuth consent, MFA being enabled, and token persistence after password reset strongly suggest OAuth token/session abuse after phishing.

questions.question: Why did password reset not fully solve the issue?
options: Password resets do not affect firewall rules
OAuth tokens/app consent can remain valid after password changes
DNS cache entries preserve account access
Mailbox forwarding rules automatically reset passwords
questions.explanation: Password reset alone may not revoke already-granted OAuth permissions or active tokens, so attacker access can persist.

questions.question: What should be revoked FIRST?
questions.explanation: The malicious OAuth consent/token is the active access path and should be revoked directly.

questions.question: What logs would help investigation most?
options: OAuth consent logs, sign-in logs, mailbox audit logs, and forwarding-rule logs
questions.explanation: OAuth consent, sign-in, mailbox audit, and forwarding-rule logs can show how access was granted, where it was used from, and what mailbox activity occurred.

questions.question: What controls could reduce similar attacks?
options: Admin consent workflow, OAuth app restrictions, phishing training, and UEBA/impossible-travel detection
questions.explanation: Restricting OAuth consent, requiring admin approval, training users, and detecting anomalous sign-ins reduce the chance and impact of OAuth phishing.

sections.title: EDR Alerts
items: No malicious files found on disk

questions.question: What type of malware behavior is MOST likely occurring?
questions.explanation: Encoded PowerShell, suspicious outbound traffic, no malicious files on disk, and disappearance after reboot indicate memory-resident or fileless malware behavior.

questions.question: Why might antivirus fail to detect it?
options: Antivirus cannot detect any PowerShell activity
The malware may avoid disk-based signatures by running mainly in memory
HTTPS traffic prevents all endpoint monitoring
questions.explanation: Fileless or memory-resident malware may evade traditional disk-based signature detection because there may be little or no malicious file to scan.

questions.question: What forensic evidence should be collected FIRST?
questions.explanation: Because the malware disappears after reboot and appears memory-resident, volatile memory should be collected before power loss or reboot.

questions.question: What containment action is MOST appropriate?
options: Isolate the affected host from the network while preserving evidence
Ignore because no file was found
questions.explanation: Isolation limits C2/exfiltration while preserving the system state for investigation.

questions.question: What indicators strongly suggest memory-resident malware?
options: High RAM usage, no disk artifacts, encoded PowerShell, suspicious outbound traffic, and disappearance after reboot
questions.explanation: The combination of volatile behavior, lack of disk artifacts, encoded PowerShell, and outbound traffic strongly supports memory-resident malware.
